We Can Reverse-Engineer SimpleView CMS

Proof of concept: /things-to-do/ section, analyzed end-to-end from CMS controls to live site output

Step 1: This is what their CMS looks like

We logged into the SimpleView CMS admin and opened the Page Builder for the /things-to-do/ page. The left panel shows every widget that makes up the page — it's a nested tree of containers and content blocks.

Step 2: What each widget actually controls

When you click "Edit" on any widget, you get a configuration form. Here's what the two most common widget types look like:

What we extracted from the controls

By opening each widget editor, we captured the complete set of options available to staff:

1
Template dropdown — determines the visual layout. The Cards widget has 6 templates: Callout Tiles (2/3/4 Across) and Cards (2/3/4 Across). The Hero widget has 4: Hero Image, Hero Image Custom, Hero Image With Caption, Hero Slideshow. The template is the key — one generic widget type renders completely differently based on which template is selected.
2
Feed dropdown — where the content comes from. Five data sources available: Nav - Primary (child pages), Events, Listings, PR - Articles, PR - Blog. On this page, the cards use Nav - Primary (pulling child pages of /things-to-do/) and the carousels use PR - Blog.
3
Filter & Sort controls — CMS Tags (Any/All), Show in Nav (Yes/No/All), Type (Page/Link), Children Of (Current/Chosen Nav Item), Sort (Created/Updated/Title/Sort Order/Random). These are query parameters that filter which content items populate the widget.
4
Template-specific toggles — each template has its own settings. Hero has "Use Blog Styles" and "Hide Text Content." Cards has "Show Background Graphic" and "More Caption" text. These map to CSS classes and render flags on the live site.

Step 3: Here's what the live site outputs

Every element on this page traces back to a specific widget in the CMS. We extracted all 19 visible widgets from the live HTML and matched them to their CMS counterparts:

CMS Widget Editor                              Live Site Output
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Hero Image (template: Hero Image)          →   Full-width pool photo
Text - Text Box (Expand Container)         →   "Things to Do in Salt Lake" H1 + intro
Related Content (feed: PR - Blog)          →   4-across blog carousel
Side-By-Side (feed: Nav - Primary)         →   "Tours & Experience Passes" split
Cards, 3 Across (feed: Nav - Primary)      →   Attractions / Great Salt Lake / Zoos...
Cards, 4 Across (feed: Nav - Primary)      →   Museums / Art Galleries / Tours...
Cards, 4 Across (feed: Nav - Primary)      →   Skiing / Hiking / Mountains...
Cards, 3 Across (feed: Nav - Primary)      →   Nightlife / Dance Clubs / Concerts...
Text Box                                   →   "See How Visitors Share" heading
Embed (raw HTML)                           →   Social media UGC feed
Marquee Side-by-Side (feed: PR - Blog)     →   "The Salt Lake Scene" featured stories
Marquee (feed: Nav - Primary)              →   State Parks / Cycling / Scenic Drives...
Text Box + FAQ Questions                   →   FAQ accordion section
Footer CTAs + Partner Logos                →   Visitor Guide / Newsletter / Experience

Step 4: How we replicate it

The CMS is simpler than it looks. Here's the key insight:

One CMS plugin type (Slides) + a template dropdown = 70% of the page

↓

We build one React component with a layout prop and it renders as cards, marquees, carousels, or side-by-sides

The full /things-to-do/ page needs only 10 React components:

  1. HeroImage — full-width image with optional caption/slideshow
  2. ContentBlock — rich text (headings, paragraphs, links)
  3. CollectionGrid — cards in 2/3/4 column layouts, sourced from a feed or manual slides
  4. SideBySide — image + text split layout
  5. RelatedContent — horizontal scrolling carousel
  6. Marquee — full-width stacked list with images
  7. MarqueeSideBySide — featured item + smaller sidebar items
  8. FAQAccordion — question/answer expandable list
  9. ActivityTabs — tabbed container grouping multiple card grids
  10. Embed — sandboxed HTML/JS injection

The admin interface would mirror what SimpleView offers: a page builder where staff pick a widget type, select a template variant, choose a feed source, and configure filters. Same mental model, modern stack.

Step 5: We script this for every page

The /things-to-do/ analysis proves we can decode one page. Here's how we scale to all 1,275:

A
Crawl the CMS sitemap — We already have the sitemap at /nav/sitemap/ with all 10 navigation sections and 1,275 pages. A script navigates to each page's Page Builder URL, extracts the widget tree (panel names, plugin types, template values, feed sources), and dumps it to JSON. Same approach we used here, automated.
B
Crawl the live site — For each page, extract all CCL widgets from the DOM: widget type, content (images, text, links, slides), configuration data attributes. Cross-reference with the CMS data to build a complete mapping.
C
Build the component catalog — As we crawl, we discover every template variant in use across the site. We expect the 10 components we identified here cover 80%+ of all pages. The remaining 20% may introduce a few more (events widgets, listing widgets, maps).
D
Generate the replacement pages — Each page becomes a JSON document: ordered array of typed sections with their configuration. A Next.js page reads this JSON and renders the matching React components. The output is visually identical to the current site.

What this looks like in practice

// Script: crawl-cms-pages.ts
// For each of the 1,275 pages in the sitemap:
//   1. Open the Page Builder URL
//   2. Extract all widget panels (name, plugin, template, feed, settings)
//   3. Open the live site URL
//   4. Extract rendered widgets (type, content, images, links)
//   5. Match CMS config → live output
//   6. Save to data/{section}/{page-slug}.json
//
// Output: complete data model for every page on the site
// Estimated runtime: ~4 hours (2 seconds per page × 1,275 pages × 2 passes)

Known Limitations

CRM data (Events, Listings, Members) — Some widgets pull from the SimpleView CRM, a completely separate ColdFusion application at simpleviewcrm.com with its own auth. We have CMS access but not CRM access. Pages like /events/ and /restaurants/ depend on CRM feeds. We'll need Visit Salt Lake to provide CRM credentials or API documentation. This does not affect /things-to-do/ — this page is 100% CMS-sourced.

Widget configuration depth — We captured the template, feed, and top-level settings for each widget. Some widgets may have additional nested configuration (e.g., specific slide items chosen via BROWSE, custom CSS overrides). The full config is accessible by opening each widget's editor — our crawl script would capture this automatically.

A/B testing & dynamic content — The footer contains A/B test panels and dynamic content based on user profiles. We can see the variants but would need to understand the targeting rules to replicate them.

BuiltByHQ — Visit Salt Lake CMS Replacement — March 2026
